unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create a polished English product for a real audience, present it clearly, respond to questions and reflect on your Grade 4 growth.
unit.learningPath: Choose evidence. Review language. Plan and draft. Improve from feedback. Present. Reflect. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 4 learner presenting a completed English portfolio

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I received a letter in the mail today.

The word mail belongs to our communication and language vocabulary.
I can explain mail in my own words.
I can connect mail to this unit's texts.
I can use mail in a clear Grade 4 sentence.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - i - l

readings.title: Review text 1: Fair Effort, Daily Gains
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Every morning, Sophia helped her mother sweep the floor and boil the water before school. She liked to stay busy, because she believed that small tasks showed big effort. She also tried to work at speed, so that she still had time to read.
At school, the head teacher pinned up a notice. “Writing Contest!” it said. “Write a report about someone in your community. The winner gets a dictionary!”
Sophia smiled. She wanted to improve her language and master new words, and a dictionary would help her learn faster. But she still had homework, chores, and her daily reading. “I must find a balance,” she told herself. “If I use my time well, I can do it all.”
That evening, her mother asked, “Can you manage this contest and your schoolwork?”
“I think so,” said Sophia. “I will try my best and continue learning.”
She thought about who to write about. There were many important people in her village, but one stood out — Mr. Mzee, the kind citizen who worked at the posthouse. He sorted and delivered the mail every day.
After class the next day, Sophia went to visit him. “Mr. Mzee,” she asked, “may I interview you for my school report?”
“Of course,” he smiled. “That sounds like a good project.”
“What do you do in your daily work?” she asked.
“I sort mail, help people send letters, and talk to visitors. I also read letters for people who cannot read well.”
“Why is mail still important?” asked Sophia.
“Because not everyone has a phone. A letter can bring peace, and it can make someone feel remembered.”
Sophia asked if he spoke more than one language. “Yes,” he said. “I had to master English and Kiswahili. Some people speak local languages too. It is necessary, so that I can help everyone.”
“And do you do this job every day?” Sophia asked.
“Yes,” he said. “It is my usual work. Some days are busy and some are quiet, but every day matters.”
Back home, Sophia began her report. She wanted it to be fair and honest, so she added all the details he had told her. She checked her spelling and used her neatest handwriting.
Her father saw her writing. “Take your time,” he said gently. “Do not leave out anything important.”
“I won't,” she replied. “This report has a purpose.”
The next morning it rained hard. A student shouted, “The contest is cancelled!” But the teacher said, “Don't worry! We will hold it in the library. Bring your reports!”
The students sat in a circle and took turns reading. Some were nervous. Others forgot their lines. Sophia waited quietly. When it was her turn, she stood up and read in a clear voice:
“Mr. Mzee is a helpful citizen who delivers the mail and helps others read. He speaks two languages and works with care. He told me that daily effort helps us gain trust and knowledge.”
After she finished, one judge asked, “What did you learn from this?”
“I learned to listen, to write clearly, and to keep going even when it rains,” Sophia said with a smile.
Later that day, the teacher made the announcement. “The winner is Sophia! Her report was honest, clear, and full of meaning.” The whole class clapped, and Sophia stood proudly, holding her prize — a brand-new dictionary.
At home, her mother hugged her. “You did it!”
“Yes,” said Sophia. “I did not stop. I worked hard, and I found a good balance.” She opened her new dictionary and pointed to a word. “Look — ‘Effort: work done with care.’ Now I know the meaning in my heart.”

readings.title: Review text 2: The Storm and the Science Tent
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Every year, Noah looked forward to the village science fair. His school set up a big tent near the lake, where students shared their projects with the whole community. This year, Noah and his friend Sophia had planned a weather display. They wanted to teach people how storms work and how to stay safe.
On the morning of the fair, the sky already looked strange. A grey, foggy mist covered the path. “I can barely see the ground,” Sophia said. “We should walk slowly.”
Noah nodded. “It's the foggiest morning we've had all year.”
When they reached the lake, the tent was ready. Their teacher, Mr Noah, was arranging the students' posters. “Set up your display near the solar light,” he said. “The sun isn't strong today, but it will still help.”
Nadia unrolled their poster about hurricanes, volcanoes and tornadoes. “This one shows a tornado moving across a field,” she said. “It spins faster than any other storm!”
Noah added, “And this picture shows a volcano with hot lava. It is dangerous, but it is also beautiful.”
People began to arrive. Some students talked about meadows, others about the lake or the forest. A younger boy pointed at Noah's display. “What is a canyon?” he asked.
“It's a deep valley,” Noah said. “Much deeper than a meadow. A river can run through it. A canyon can be the deepest land on Earth.”
Mr Noah clapped his hands. “Time for a safety quiz! Sophia, your turn.”
Nadia stood up. “What causes a hurricane?” she asked.
A girl raised her hand. “Warm water and strong wind?”
“Correct!” Sophia smiled.
“Now Noah,” said the teacher.
Noah pointed to a picture. “Where does hail fall from?”
“From the clouds during a storm,” a student answered.
“Right,” said Noah. “And hail can damage crops, so we must always watch the weather.”
Just then, the wind picked up. The surface of the lake began to ripple. A few parents looked up at the sky.
Suddenly, a man shouted, “It's coming back! The storm from last week!”
Some children began to panic. “Don't run!” Mr Noah said firmly. “Let's all move inside the hall. Quickly, but calmly.”
As they packed their things, Noah felt something cold on his cheek. “Hail!” he said. Small balls of ice bounced on the ground. “Let's go!”
They reached the hall just as the wind began to howl outside. Mr Noah closed the door. “We're safe here,” he said.
Nadia sat down, catching her breath. “My heart is beating fast,” she said. Her shirt was wet with sweat.
“That was scary,” said Noah. “But it was also kind of exciting.”
Inside, they carried on with their weather talk. They showed how animals use thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather, and how moisture in the soil helps plants grow.
“Rust forms when metal gets wet and is left too long,” Sophia explained. “Like that old nail outside.”
“And mold grows on food when we leave it too long,” Noah added. “That's why we always cover our lunch boxes!”
One girl pointed at a chart. “What's that tall tree near the bay?” she asked.
“That's a royal palm tree,” Sophia said. “It grows near the water. It is tall and strong, like a king's tree.”
Noah smiled. “Nature is powerful,” he said, “but we can learn to understand it.”
When the storm finally passed, the sky cleared. Sunshine filled the room. “Look,” a student said. “The light is shining brightly now.”
Mr Noah smiled. “You all did well today. You learned, you taught, and you stayed calm. You showed real courage.”
Noah and Sophia packed up their project. “Even with the storm, this was the most exciting science fair ever,” Noah said.
“And we didn't even have to roam far to learn something new,” Sophia laughed.
Outside, puddles sparkled in the sun. The wind had slowed — but the lesson had stayed.

readings.title: Review text 3: The Bitter Lunch
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In the hot fields of Sudan, Musa helped his uncle grow rice and care for the cattle. Every morning before school, he fed the cows and carried water from the well. It was hard labor, but his uncle always smiled and said, “We work hard now to eat well later.”
One day at school, Musa's teacher stood at the front of the class. “I have a project for you,” she said. “Each of you will write a story about food, and it must carry a health message. You must use our new words and write with care. You might even share your story at the community lunch.”
Musa raised his hand. “Can it be a true story?” he asked.
“Yes,” the teacher said, “as long as it teaches something helpful.”
That evening, Musa walked to the village bakery. It smelled of warm bread. He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.
“I should tell you,” said the baker, “this meat is not fresh. But it still tastes good.” Musa was too hungry to care.
At home, he shared part of the sandwich with his cousin Sarah. She frowned. “I think this meat is from sick cattle,” she said. “There is a strange smell.”
“You worry too much,” said Musa, and he ate the rest.
Late that night, Musa's stomach hurt badly. He felt heat in his belly and cold in his hands, and he could not sleep. Sarah heard him groaning and woke her father.
At the health center, a kind nurse checked him carefully. “This may be food poisoning,” she said. “Some chemicals are used to clean old meat, and they can be dangerous if they are not washed off.”
Musa groaned. “I only ate a sandwich. It had a lot of spice, but the meat may have been old.”
“You must be more careful,” said the nurse gently. “Old meat and dirty tools can lead to disease. Eaten too often, bad food can even lead to a serious sickness like cancer. Always check your food first.”
The next day, Musa stayed home from school. His uncle brought him warm soup and said, “You are lucky this was not deadly. Always check what you eat.”
By the afternoon, Musa felt better. Then he remembered his story. He sat outside with his notebook and wrote:
“I once ate a bad sandwich. It looked fine, but it was not fresh. It gave me a sore stomach, and I learned to check what I eat.”
He thought for a while, then wrote more: “Some chemicals help to clean food, but too much is harmful. Farmers must use less pesticide, and people must wash their food with care.”
“What else should I write?” he asked Sarah.
“Gather some facts about food and health,” she said. “Then give good advice.”
Musa smiled. “That helps.” He added a final part: “We eat to feed our brain and our body, and good food helps us grow. Too much alcohol or dirty food can make us sick. A famine shows us how precious food really is, and how we must protect it.”
On the day of the community lunch, Musa read his story aloud. A few children laughed at first — until he reached the part about his illness. Then the room grew quiet, and everyone listened.
“Thank you for that, Musa,” said the teacher. “Now, who else would like to share?”
Hannah raised her hand. “Musa also learned where to put a comma in a list of food!” The whole room giggled.
Then the village elder stood up. “Food is a gift from Allah,” he said. “Let us not waste it, and let us never eat without checking it first.”
As the food was served — lamb, rice, and sweet dates — Musa chose only what looked clean and safe. The meal was simple, but everything was warm and fresh.
Musa leaned over to Sarah. “This is the freshest lamb I have had in weeks,” he whispered.
She grinned. “That is because I helped to cook it.”
Musa took a bite and smiled. This time, his stomach did not hurt at all.

readings.title: Review text 4: The Circular Plan
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small town near the coast of Ghana. Her school was plain on the outside, but it was full of energy inside. Every week, the teacher gave the class a new challenge — something to learn or build together as a team.
One morning, the teacher wrote on the board: “Project: Create a way to share information with the community.”
Amal raised her hand. “Like a newspaper?”
“Something like that,” the teacher said. “But you must use what you already know. Use your knowledge, and make something useful.”
The students talked during break. “We could build a big sign!” said Ishmael.
“No,” said Selma. “People will just walk past it. Let's make a box with papers inside.”
“That's still too plain,” Amal added. “It has to be circular. That's my rule!”
“Why circular?” Ishmael teased. “Is your brain a wheel?” Amal laughed, but she did not answer. She already had an idea.
After school, Amal went to the local library. She looked at the location of every fiction shelf, every news board, and every reading chair. She found a dusty corner near the door and smiled. “There is space here,” she whispered.
The librarian came over. “Can I help you?”
“We want to deliver news and stories to the whole town,” Amal replied.
“That's a big job,” the librarian said kindly. “But good service always starts small.”
The next day, Amal and her friends arrived early. They brought cardboard, tape, scissors, and markers.
“What are you building?” asked a younger boy.
“A circular news station!” Amal answered. “People will stop here and spin the circle. Each part gives different information — the weather, stories, or health tips.”
Ishmael frowned. “That sounds hard.”
“It's a challenge,” Amal agreed. “But we can do it.”
The teacher came by and nodded. “Very creative,” she said. “Now check your spelling before you stick anything down. And don't forget to erase the pencil lines.”
The group worked neatly. Amal drew the circle. Ishmael wrote the headlines. Selma added a funny comic about a scientist and a dancing goat, and everyone laughed.
“We need facts too,” Amal said, “not just fiction.”
“There is a doctor in town,” said Ishmael. “Let's write about health.”
“There are new shops opening as well,” added Selma.
“Write clearly. That is our top priority,” Amal reminded them.
By Friday, the project was ready. They placed it near the school gate. It stood tall and colourful.
The next morning, a group of parents and children gathered around it. “What is this?” asked a man.
“It's called ‘The Circular Plan',” Amal explained. “Turn it, and read what's on each side.”
A woman read aloud, “Tips for clean water.” A boy spun it and found the comic. “A goat that becomes a scientist?” he laughed.
“It's just fiction!” Ishmael shouted, grinning. People laughed and clapped. “This is good service,” someone said. “Our children are teaching us.”
One parent pointed. “But there is no news about the market. That's what people care about.”
“Then please help us write it!” Amal replied. “We want real thought from the people too.”
Another woman smiled. “That's a good priority — sharing everyone's voice.”
The teacher stood beside the project. “This,” she said, “is how we build a community. We learn, we teach, and we grow. Today, our students became leaders.”
As the crowd left, a boy began to tease Ishmael about his handwriting. “Your words look like little bugs!” he laughed. Ishmael grinned. “I wrote them in a hurry. I'll fix them later.”
“Good idea,” said Amal. “We must always check for quality.”
That afternoon, Amal looked at the circle again. “It may look plain,” she said to Selma, “but the work inside it matters.”
Samira nodded. “It's not about how big the quantity is,” she said. “It's about what people learn.”
And from that day on, people in Amal's town stopped by the spinning circle, learned something new, and sometimes even added their own page.

readings.title: Review text 5: The Spiral Cave
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One afternoon, in a quiet village near the forest, three children — Ibrahim, Isaac, and Musa — were playing beside the river. They often made up games and told stories, but today they found something strange. Behind a pile of rocks, there was a dark hole in the ground.
“Let me peek inside!” said Isaac.
Musa held his hand. “Be careful. It might not be safe.”
But Ibrahim had already taken a step forward. “It is dark, but I can see something. The floor goes down in a spiral.”
“Like a staircase?” asked Isaac.
“Yes! Let us explore it!” said Ibrahim, feeling excited.
The children looked around for a strong stick and a torch. “We must check that it is safe,” said Musa. “We do not want to fall.”
They went in slowly. “First, peek inside. Then, proceed carefully,” whispered Ibrahim, copying what their teacher always said in science class.
Inside, the air was cold. The spiral tunnel turned and turned. Isaac pointed to the wall. “Look! A drawing of people… and a lion?”
They kept walking until they reached a wider space. “It is a cave!” shouted Musa. Their voices echoed off the stone.
Suddenly, they heard a soft sound.
“Was that a whistle?” asked Ibrahim.
“No,” said Isaac. “That was an animal!”
Then, out of the shadows, two eyes appeared. A small dog — thin and shivering — stepped forward. It wagged its tail.
“It looks hungry,” said Musa. “We have to rescue it!”
Ibrahim reached into his bag and pulled out a piece of bread. He called gently to the dog. “Come here, boy.”
The dog sniffed and walked closer. “I think he is friendly,” said Isaac. “He does not want to hurt us.”
“We should give him a name,” said Ibrahim.
“Not yet,” said Musa. “First we need to check that he is not sick.”
The children carried the dog out of the cave. Outside, the sun was low in the sky.
“We must accelerate,” said Isaac. “It is getting late!”
Back in the village, they went to the animal helper, Mama Hannah. She was kind and wise.
“Where did you find him?” she asked.
“In a spiral tunnel near the river,” said Ibrahim.
“You were lucky,” said Mama Hannah. “That tunnel is very old. There is pressure from the soil above it. It could fall in at any time.”
“We did not know,” said Musa. “We were too excited.”
“Next time you must tell your parents first,” she said. “But I am glad you brought him to me.”
The dog was given clean water and food. He wagged his tail and let Musa squeeze his paw gently.
“He is strong,” said Mama Hannah. “He only needs rest.”
“But who left him there?” asked Isaac.
“We may never know,” said Mama Hannah. “Sometimes people are greedy. They want an animal but do not care for it. That is not right. An animal like this can suffer when no one looks after it.”
The children were quiet. They did not like the thought of anyone being so unkind.
“We can help him,” said Musa. “Let us make posters and describe him. Someone might be looking for him.”
“That is a kind plan,” said Mama Hannah.
That night, Ibrahim wrote in his notebook:
“We found a cave, a spiral path, and a small dog. We learned to move slowly and to think. We felt pressure and fear, but we stayed strong.”
The next day, the children shared their story at school. Their teacher smiled. “You followed instructions, stayed calm, and made good choices. That is something to be proud of.”
“What should we name him now?” asked Isaac.
“How about the rescued dog?” said Musa. “He is strong, like a lion.”
“Perfect,” said Ibrahim. “He is our little rescued lion.”
the rescued dog barked — happy, and safe at last.

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

writing.title: Portfolio captions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a precise paragraph about each of three selected pieces and explain what each shows.
writing.modelText: This revised report shows that I can organise facts and support an explanation with evidence.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Project proposal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your topic, audience, product and plan in two organised paragraphs.
writing.modelText: My project will teach younger learners about caring for nature. I will create an illustrated guide using facts and examples.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: First draft
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write the complete first draft of your capstone product.
writing.modelText: Use a clear opening, organised paragraphs, evidence or examples, and a conclusion suited to your audience.
writing.sentenceStarter: I want my audience to understand...
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Revision from feedback
writing.promptAndInstructions: Record two feedback points and rewrite the sections they improve.
writing.modelText: Feedback asked for stronger evidence, so I added a detail from Unit 7 and explained why it matters.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Presentation notes
writing.promptAndInstructions: Prepare concise cue cards for a three-minute presentation.
writing.modelText: Opening; three main ideas; evidence; audience question; closing.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Grade 4 reflection
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your strongest progress, one challenge, and a specific Grade 5 goal.
writing.modelText: I became a stronger reader because I learned to compare evidence. Next, I will improve paragraph transitions.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback

quizzes.question: What does 'mail' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there. | Letters and packages that are sent or delivered. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered.
quizzes.explanation: I received a letter in the mail today.

quizzes.question: What does 'language' mean?
quizzes.options: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings. | To get something you want, or to become better at something. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings.
quizzes.explanation: English is a language that many people speak around the world.

quizzes.question: What does 'breath' mean?
quizzes.options: A small amount of water in the air or on a surface. | The air you take in and let out of your lungs. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The air you take in and let out of your lungs.
quizzes.explanation: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.

quizzes.question: What does 'sweat' mean?
quizzes.options: A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom. | The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot.
quizzes.explanation: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.

quizzes.explanation: The text says: He sorted and delivered the mail every day.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 3: The Bitter Lunch': He was hungry, so he bought a _____ filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 3: The Bitter Lunch': “Let us not waste it, and let us never eat without checking it first.” As the food was served — _____, rice, and sweet dates — Musa chose only what looked clean and safe.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Let us not waste it, and let us never eat without checking it first.” As the food was served — lamb, rice, and sweet dates — Musa chose only what looked clean and safe.

Letters and packages that are sent or delivered. I received a letter in the mail today.
A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings. English is a language that many people speak around the world.
The air you take in and let out of your lungs. I took a deep breath before I started to speak.
The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot. After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.
Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside. I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
A young sheep, or the meat from it. We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
Help or work done for others. The library started a free book service for the town.
To bring something to a person or place. The mail carrier will deliver our letters.
To run fast on four legs, like a horse. The horse began to gallop across the field.
To go faster, or to make something go faster. The car can accelerate when the driver presses the pedal.
